description:
## Why
When an ask condition gives an inconsistent verdict (the main ask flakiness source), the system currently has no adaptive behavior — it immediately re-asks with the same prompt. Progressive backoff with evidence injection improves accuracy on retry.

## What
Track consecutive ask failures for a node in NodeProgress. On retry:
- Attempt 1: Re-ask with previous verdict and grounded-by output as additional context
- Attempt 2: Switch to simplified yes/no forced-choice format with explicit binary instructions
- Attempt 3: Default to conservative answer (false for while-ask, true for until-ask), emit WARNING

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceConditionLoop), src/domain/judge-prompt.ts (new retry prompt builders).

## Acceptance criteria
- Consecutive failure count tracked in NodeProgress
- Three retry tiers implemented with distinct prompts
- Conservative default applied at tier 3 with logged warning
- Unit tests for each retry tier
- Smoke tests with ask conditions pass
title: Add ask-condition progressive backoff: inject evidence and simplify on retry

description:
Why:
The next useful proof point is not another synthetic example but real workflows in real projects. Dogfooding prompt-language in external repos should reveal missing primitives, portability issues, prompt ergonomics problems, and where the language actually saves time versus just looking clever in demos.

What:
- Pilot prompt-language in concrete external projects owned by the user, starting with `llm-quant` and `portriam`
- For each pilot, identify one high-value workflow that is repetitive enough to justify a flow, then test whether prompt-language helps in practice
- Explore an OpenClaw integration path as a separate host/runtime spike, with explicit attention to security, approval surfaces, and lifecycle differences
- Capture reusable lessons: what should become docs, examples, features, or product-boundary decisions inside this repo

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Each target project has a scoped pilot bead with a concrete workflow to test
- OpenClaw exploration yields a documented first-path recommendation or explicit reasons to defer
- Cross-project learnings are captured as follow-up docs/issues
title: Dogfood prompt-language on real projects and external hosts

description:
## Why
`llm-quant` is a good candidate for real-world dogfooding if it has repeatable workflows such as research synthesis, backtest prep, experiment review, or guardrailed multi-step coding tasks. A real pilot will expose whether prompt-language helps outside controlled fixtures.

## What
1. Pick one concrete `llm-quant` workflow worth encoding as a prompt-language flow
2. Map the workflow steps, required variables, gates, and any repo-specific context files
3. Implement a first pilot flow in that repo (or a scratch branch/worktree if safer)
4. Record what worked, what was awkward, and which missing features or docs gaps appeared

## Acceptance criteria
- One named `llm-quant` workflow selected and scoped
- Pilot flow authored and exercised at least once
- Findings captured as docs or follow-up beads in this repo
title: [RESEARCH] llm-quant pilot: validate one real workflow with prompt-language

description:

## What
1. Identify one repetitive `portriam` workflow that would benefit from explicit sequencing and gates
2. Design a small pilot flow for that workflow
3. Run the pilot and capture friction points: context loading, command integration, gate design, and portability
4. Convert any repeated pain points into product/docs beads here

## Acceptance criteria
- One concrete `portriam` workflow selected
- Pilot flow tested on the target repo
- Product/docs follow-ups created for meaningful gaps
title: [RESEARCH] portriam pilot: validate one real workflow with prompt-language

description:
## Why
OpenClaw is the most interesting non-Claude host in the current research set because it has durable agents, plugins, approvals, and a more always-on operational model. An integration spike would test whether prompt-language should remain session-scoped, become host-adaptable, or stop at advisory integration.

## What
1. Identify the smallest credible OpenClaw integration path: wrapper flow runner, plugin, prompt asset, or host-interop shim
2. Map lifecycle differences versus Claude/Codex: approvals, durable state, scheduled execution, plugin model, and sandbox expectations
3. Define the security posture for any integration proposal, especially around skills/plugins, approvals, and secret handling
4. Produce a recommendation: prototype now, track as future work, or defer

## Acceptance criteria
- Integration options and constraints documented
- Security and product-boundary implications called out explicitly
- Clear next-step recommendation produced
title: [RESEARCH] OpenClaw integration spike: first runnable path, constraints, and security model

description:
## Why
Issue backlogs accumulate cruft over time — duplicate issues, superseded ideas, issues that became irrelevant after implementation. Regular grooming keeps the backlog actionable and morale high.

## What (recurring — run monthly)
1. Run: bd stale — identify issues with no activity in 60+ days
2. Run: bd orphans — identify issues with broken dependencies
3. Review all P3/P4 issues: are they still relevant? Close anything superseded
4. Identify 3 quick-win issues (high impact, low cost, no blockers) to highlight for the next sprint
5. Review all in_progress issues: are any stalled? Flag for human decision
6. Check for duplicate issues created by separate sessions
7. Update priority on any issues that have become more/less urgent

## Acceptance criteria
- All stale issues resolved (closed, deferred, or commented with status update)
- All orphan dependencies fixed
- 3 quick wins identified and labeled
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance monthly.
title: [QA] Backlog grooming: close stale issues, reprioritize, identify quick wins

description:
Why: The post-tool-use hook fires for all tools but can't inject targeted guidance. Tool-specific context is critical for reliability — after a Bash call, remind the AI to check exit codes; after Write, remind it to validate.

What: New `on-tool:` section in the DSL. When a specified tool fires post-use, inject associated text as additionalContext.

Example:
```
on-tool:
  Bash: "Remember to check exit codes and run tests after shell commands"
  Write: "Verify the written file matches the spec before continuing"
  Edit: "Run the test suite to verify your change didn't break anything"
```

Acceptance criteria:
- on-tool section parsed and stored in FlowSpec
- post-tool-use hook checks toolName against on-tool map
- Matching text injected as additionalContext
- No match: no injection (backward compatible)
title: Tool-aware post-tool-use context injection: `on-tool` DSL section

description:
Why: All spawned children use the default model. Many sub-tasks (file listing, simple transforms, boilerplate generation) don't need the most capable model. Cost optimization is critical for multi-agent flows — Haiku is 5-10x cheaper and faster for simple tasks.

What: Add optional `model` parameter to spawn nodes. ClaudeProcessSpawner passes --model <model-id> to the child claude -p invocation. Support aliases: 'haiku', 'sonnet', 'opus'.

Example:
```
spawn "list-files" model "haiku"
  let files = prompt "List all test files"
end
spawn "fix-auth" model "sonnet"
  prompt: Fix the authentication bug
end
await all
```

Acceptance criteria:
- model parameter accepted in parser
- Correct --model flag passed to claude -p
- Invalid model names fail with clear error at spawn time
- No model specified: use default (backward compatible)
title: Model selection for spawn: `spawn "name" model "haiku"`

description:
Why: Current adaptive polling (1s/2s/5s/10s steps) has two issues: (1) multiple concurrent await nodes create synchronized polling storms, (2) total timeout is derived from poll count (150 * interval) making it hard to predict. With jitter, concurrent polls naturally desynchronize. With wall-clock timeout, behavior is predictable.

What: Replace computePollInterval step function with exponential backoff + jitter: min(base * 2^attempt + random(0, base), maxInterval). Add configurable wall-clock total timeout for await (default: 25 min).

Acceptance criteria:
- Multiple concurrent awaits don't poll in lockstep
- Total timeout is wall-clock based, not poll-count based
- Configurable via PROMPT_LANGUAGE_AWAIT_TIMEOUT env var
- Existing await tests still pass
title: Spawn await: exponential backoff + jitter, wall-clock timeout

description:
## Why
The DSL evolves organically. Without periodic review, footguns accumulate, syntax becomes inconsistent, and the onboarding experience degrades. An experienced PL designer reviewing the DSL from scratch surfaces issues that are invisible to daily contributors.

## What (recurring — run every 6 months or before a major version)
1. Read the DSL cheatsheet cold — does it make sense without prior knowledge?
2. Write 3 non-trivial flows from scratch using only the docs — where did you get stuck?
3. Review the known footguns (stringly-typed, globals, no precedence, no scoping)
4. Are there any primitives that are missing and frequently worked around?
5. Review the lint warnings — are they helpful or noisy?
6. Review the error messages when flows fail — are they actionable?
7. Check: is the 'ask' keyword intuitive or confusing?
8. Review the flow rendering output — does it clearly show current position?

## Acceptance criteria
- At least 3 findings filed as issues or improvements
- DSL cheatsheet updated if any inaccuracies found
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance every 6 months.
title: [REVIEW] DSL ergonomics review: footguns, onboarding friction, and missing primitives

description:
## Why
Conditions like ${name} > 5 fail silently when name is a string. The condition evaluator returns null (unknown) and falls through to command execution which also fails. This is one of the most confusing runtime behaviors — it silently does nothing.

## What
In lintFlow(), add a condition type checker:
- Parse condition expressions at lint time
- If a ${var} reference with >, <, >=, <= has a declared string type (from let name: string), warn
- If a variable initialized with a string literal (let name = 'alice') is used with comparison operators, warn
- If both sides of == are string literals, info-level note about string equality

Reuse extractVarRefs() and collectDefinedVariables() from the existing linter.

Key files: src/domain/lint-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Warns when string-typed var used with >, <, >=, <=
- Warns when string-literal-initialized var used with numeric comparison
- No false positives on numeric-initialized vars
- Works with gradual typing annotations when implemented
title: Add condition type checking to linter (warn on comparison with wrong types)

description:
## Why this issue exists

The generator-evaluator pattern from the Anthropic harness blog is achievable today with spawn/await, but there's no documentation or formula showing users how to do it. This creates friction for the most powerful multi-agent use case.

## What needs to be done

Add a `bd formula` template and a docs/examples entry showing the debate pattern with current DSL:

```
# generator.flow
flow:
  let feedback = ${feedback:-No feedback yet}
  prompt: Build feature X. Previous feedback: ${feedback}

# evaluator.flow  
flow:
  let work_summary = ${work_summary}
  run: npm test
  ask "Is the implementation complete and correct?"
    grounded-by: npm test
  let approved = ${__judge_....__}
  let critique = prompt "What specifically needs improvement?"

# orchestrator.flow
flow:
  let approved = false
  while not ${approved} == true max 5
    spawn "generator"
    await "generator"
    spawn "evaluator"
    await "evaluator"
    let approved = ${evaluator.approved}
    let feedback = ${evaluator.critique}
  end
```

Key things to document:
- Variables flow parent→child automatically on spawn
- Child output variables are imported with name prefix (evaluator.approved)
- Feedback variable from evaluator becomes input to next generator spawn
- Skeptical evaluator prompting (via evaluator's flow prompt text)

Also document the limitations today vs what prompt-language-s6zz would add.
title: Recipe: multi-agent debate pattern using spawn/await

description:
Why:
The Codex CLI port needs the same level of evidence as the Claude path: tests, CI-grade checks, smoke tests, and experiment runs. The goal is not just "it runs" but "it produces materially the same conclusions as the existing prompt-language experiments unless there is a documented host/runtime reason for divergence."

What:
- Define the authoritative Codex validation matrix across `npm run test`, `npm run ci`, `npm run eval:smoke`, and the experiment scripts that matter for parity.
- Run the matrix on a supported Codex environment (Linux/macOS/WSL, not native Windows while hooks remain unsupported there).
- Compare Codex outcomes with the existing Claude/experiment baseline: pass/fail status, smoke coverage, comparative win/loss/tie patterns, and any host-specific caveats.
- File follow-up bug/issues for any regressions, unsupported scenarios, or result deltas that cannot be explained by known host differences.
- Document the conclusions in repo docs or evaluation notes so future comparisons have a stable baseline.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Codex-specific validation matrix is defined and checked into the repo or captured in issue notes
- Tests/CI/smoke/eval runs executed on a supported Codex setup
- Deltas versus existing baseline are explicitly classified as parity, expected variance, or bug
- Follow-up issues created for unresolved gaps
notes:
QA blocker observed in this workspace: claude -p \ Say hello\ fails with 'Your organization does not have access to Claude. Please login again or contact your administrator.' As a result, scripts/eval/smoke-test.mjs currently reports 0/41 passed with empty outputs rather than a single actionable blocker. Tracking a preflight/harness fix separately so parity runs fail fast when auth is missing.
Confirmed on 2026-04-09: npm run eval:smoke still blocks immediately in this workspace because Claude auth/login is unavailable. Build and install succeed, but smoke scenarios do not run. This remains an environment/parity blocker, not a repo regression.
title: Codex CLI: parity run against tests and experiment baseline

description:
Why: On compaction/resume, autoAdvanceNodes restarts from currentNodePath which may point to a node that was in progress but not completed. This can cause partial re-execution of the node the path points to.

What: Add lastCompletedNodePath to SessionState, updated atomically after each successful node advancement. On resume, start from lastCompletedNodePath + 1 rather than currentNodePath.

Acceptance criteria:
- lastCompletedNodePath always points to a fully-completed node
- Resume starts at the correct next node, never replays a completed one
- Handles edge case: first node has never completed (start from beginning)
- Atomic update prevents split-brain between currentNodePath and lastCompletedNodePath
title: Checkpoint/resume granularity: track lastCompletedNodePath to prevent partial re-execution

description:
## Why
The parser (parse-flow.ts) is an ad-hoc regex + state machine. It handles malformed input via soft-fail (warns not errors). Property-based testing with random/adversarial inputs will surface edge cases that unit tests miss — especially around indentation handling, special characters in strings, and deeply nested structures.

## What
Use fast-check (already a common vitest companion) to generate:
1. Random valid flow strings constructed from grammar rules — parse must not throw
2. Random indentation variations — parse must produce consistent structure
3. Strings with shell special chars in prompts (quotes, backticks, $, !) — parse must not corrupt
4. Deeply nested flows (10+ levels) — parse must not stack overflow
5. Very long variable names and values — parse must truncate or warn gracefully
6. Unicode in prompt text — parse must handle correctly

Add as parse-flow.property.test.ts alongside existing parse-flow tests.

## Acceptance criteria
- At least 5 property-based test suites implemented
- All pass with 1000 generated examples
- Any failures become regression unit tests
title: [QA] Add property-based tests for the DSL parser (fuzz parseFlow)

description:
## Why
For high-stakes ask conditions (e.g., 'is this safe to deploy?'), a single Claude judgment has ~10-15% error rate. Multi-sample voting reduces error rate from p to 3p^2 - 2p^3 — for 10% base rate, drops to ~2.8%.

## What
Add optional 'vote N' suffix to ask conditions:
  while ask 'is the code production-ready?' vote 3

This emits N separate judge prompts in sequence, each writing to __judge_{nodeId}_1__, _2, _3. Takes majority vote. N must be odd. Default behavior (no vote) unchanged — single judgment.

Key files: src/application/parse-flow.ts (parse 'vote N'), src/domain/flow-node.ts (add voteCount to ConditionLoopNode/IfNode), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceConditionLoop), src/domain/judge-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- 'vote N' syntax parsed and stored
- N judge prompts emitted sequentially
- Majority vote taken correctly
- N must be odd (lint error if even)
- Adds N turns overhead (documented tradeoff)
title: Add opt-in multi-sample voting for ask conditions (ask '...' vote 3)

description: Codex CLI now has hooks, plugins, skills, and a non-interactive `codex exec` mode, so prompt-language can be ported with high reuse across domain and application layers. The realistic target is near-Claude parity on macOS/Linux/WSL, not exact parity. Codex-specific gaps from the current March 28, 2026 docs: no `TaskCompleted` event, no `PreCompact` event, `UserPromptSubmit` adds `additionalContext` instead of replacing the prompt, and `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` currently only emit `Bash`. Hooks are still experimental and currently disabled on native Windows. Gate enforcement therefore needs to move into `Stop`, prompt injection needs a Codex-specific adapter, and write/edit-driven UX needs a Bash-only fallback. Parent epic tracking the full Codex CLI port.

description:
## Why
In long conversations (20+ turns), Claude's attention to early system instructions degrades. The capture format instruction gets buried. Adding a brief reminder in the flow visualization (injected every turn) reinforces the instruction at the right moment.

## What
In renderFlow() (render-flow.ts), when a node is in awaiting_capture state, append a one-liner reminder below the flow visualization:

  [Capture active: write response to .prompt-language/vars/{varName} using Write tool]

This costs ~15 tokens per turn when capture is active and ensures the instruction appears at the end of context (most attended by the model).

Key files: src/domain/render-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Reminder line appears in render output when capture is active
- Reminder correctly shows variable name
- Only shown when awaiting_capture (not on every turn)
- Unit tests for render output with/without active capture
title: Recency-boost capture instructions: add active-capture reminder to flow rendering

description:
Why: Each flow starts from scratch. For iterative development, there's no way to carry forward what the AI learned. Memory enables: learning from failures, cross-session context, progressive refinement across multiple runs.

What: `memory:` section that persists key-value pairs across flow executions. Stored in .prompt-language/memory.json. New `remember "key" = ${value}` node saves to memory. ${memory.key} reads from memory.

Example:
```
flow:
  prompt: Fix the bug. Previously we tried: ${memory.last_approach}
  let approach = prompt "What approach are you taking?"
  remember "last_approach" = ${approach}
```

Acceptance criteria:
- memory.json persists across flow completions and session restarts
- remember node saves at advancement time
- ${memory.key} resolves in interpolation
- Clear mechanism: bd flow-memory clear or manual delete
- TTL option to prevent stale memory misleading AI
title: Persistent memory across flows: `remember` / `memory:` section

description:
Why: Multi-way branching with if/elif/elif/.../else is verbose and error-prone when switching on a single variable value. This is common after `let status = run '...'`.

```
  case "success"

  case "retry"
    prompt: Let's try again
  default
    prompt: Unexpected status: ${status}
end
```

Acceptance criteria:

- match and case are not existing keywords — pure addition
- Works with variable and literal value matching

description:
## Why
'let x += prompt text' inside foreach is the highest-flakiness pattern. If each capture has 90% reliability, 5 iterations drops overall reliability to 59% (0.9^5). Each iteration requires a full capture cycle.

## What
When the system detects 'let x += prompt' inside a foreach body, batch the capture: emit a single prompt listing all N items, ask Claude to respond to each, write all responses as a newline-separated list to the capture file. This converts N sequential capture cycles into 1.

Implementation requires:
- Detecting the foreach+append-capture pattern at advancement time
- Building a batched prompt from all remaining iteration items
- Parsing the multi-line response and splitting into individual list entries
- Advancing past all iterations in one step

This is a significant change to advanceForeachEntry() and capture semantics. Only viable when prompts are independent across iterations (no per-item state dependency).

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceForeachEntry, advanceLetPrompt), src/domain/capture-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Foreach+append-capture pattern detected
- Batched prompt emitted for independent iterations
- Multi-line response correctly split and appended
- Falls back to sequential capture when iterations have dependencies
- Smoke test T (list accumulation in foreach) passes consistently
title: Implement foreach capture batching to fix let-append-prompt inside loops

description:
Why: A spawned claude -p process can crash (OOM, SIGKILL) without updating its state file. The parent then polls 150 times over ~25 minutes waiting for a dead process. With PID liveness checking, the parent detects the crash within one poll cycle.

What: In advanceAwaitNode(), before polling state file, check if child PID is still alive using process.kill(pid, 0). If PID is dead but state shows 'running' or state doesn't exist, immediately mark child as failed.

Acceptance criteria:
- Dead child process detected within one poll cycle
- Parent flow proceeds to catch/fail path immediately
- On Windows: skip PID check (same pattern as isLockStale)
- ~15 LOC change
title: Spawn PID liveness check: fail fast when spawned child crashes without updating state

description:
Why: prompt-language currently orchestrates a single hardcoded Claude Code harness with inline flow text. Users want stronger control over injected context, reusable context bundles, named sub-agents, reusable flow imports, and a first-class flow-file workflow that can launch through a preferred agent harness.

Scope:
- Reusable context profiles that can bundle skills, memory/context files, system preamble, model hints, and tool policy
- Named agent definitions that map spawn targets to a harness plus a default profile
- Static composition of flow files via import (reuse existing bead prompt-language-jkfn)
- Harness abstraction so agent execution is not permanently tied to claude -p
- First-class flow-file execution and default-runner configuration
- OS-level installer/UX for file association and launch behavior
- Guided configuration UX (command/skill) for setting up harnesses, profiles, and file associations

Non-goals:
- OO/method-call DSL syntax such as agent.skillLoad(...).prompt(...)
- Arbitrary function imports into the DSL in the first iteration
- Baking Claude-specific skill semantics directly into the core AST

Architecture direction:
Keep the DSL declarative. Treat skills as one possible backend capability inside a broader profile/context-pack model so the same DSL can target multiple harnesses over time.
title: Profiles, agents, imports, and runner UX for prompt-language

description:

description:

## What

- prompt, run, while, until, retry, if, try, foreach, let, break, continue, spawn, await

For each node kind, identify which smoke test(s) primarily exercise it. If any node kind has no primary smoke test (only incidental coverage), create a new targeted smoke test scenario.

Also verify the ask keyword has a dedicated smoke test (it was added in Phase 4).

## Acceptance criteria
- Coverage matrix documented (node kind → smoke test ID)
- Any gaps have new smoke test scenarios added to smoke-test.mjs
- Total smoke test count updated in CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md if new tests added

description:
Why: Production flows need credentials (API keys, database passwords). Current approach (env vars) works but is fragile and unauditable. Vault integration adds security and auditability.

What: secrets: section in flow spec or .prompt-language/config.json:
```yaml
secrets:
  API_KEY:
    source: env  # from environment variable
  DB_PASSWORD:
    source: op   # 1Password: op read 'op://vault/item/field'
```

Resolved secrets injected into run: node environments (NOT into variables — excluded from state and audit logs).

Acceptance criteria:
- env source reads from process environment
- op source spawns 1Password CLI (op read)
- Secrets never written to state file or audit log
- filterSpawnVariables() prevents credential leak to children
- 1Password CLI not required (only if op source used)
title: Secrets management: resolve credentials from env/1Password/Vault at flow start

description:
Why:
The language already has real variable machinery (`let`/`var`, built-in auto-vars, foreach loop vars, child vars after `await`), but the design questions are still muddy: what counts as a variable definition, whether undefined variables are acceptable, whether scope should stay flat, and whether richer data structures should exist.

What:
- Inspect the shipped variable model across parser, AST, session state, interpolation, advancement, rendering, and docs.
- Separate current behavior from proposed enhancements.
- Identify which open variable-related beads are true feature gaps versus documentation or tooling gaps.
- Produce a short design/research note that becomes the reference point for future variable work.

Related open beads to consider:
- prompt-language-h2an (/flow-vars skill)
- prompt-language-cva6 (const keyword)
- prompt-language-nm4l and prompt-language-brwd (shadow warnings)
- prompt-language-mewj (structured map variables)
- prompt-language-s5cx (typed variable declarations)
notes: QA found a validator gap: examples that use memory-prefetched keys and remember/runtime-introduced vars still emit 'Reference to undefined variable' warnings (for example helpdesk-sdlc/project.flow warns on helpdesk_target_market and preferred_package_manager). The issue is likely lint semantics, not the example flows.
title: [RESEARCH] Variable model: semantics, scoping, and data structures

acceptance_criteria:
- Current declaration and lookup semantics documented precisely
- Recommendation given for undefined vars, declaration requirements, and shadowing policy

description:
Question set:
- How are variables defined today: parser forms, runtime initialization paths, built-in auto-vars, foreach vars, and child-variable import?
- Do variables need explicit prior definition, or is current late-bound interpolation acceptable?
- What should happen with undefined variables, fallback syntax, reassignment, and shadowing?
- Should the language remain flat/global or move toward lexical/block scope?

What:
- Document current semantics with code references.
- Compare current flat global model against a lint-only model and a lexical-scope model.
- Recommend whether declaration rules belong in parser, linter, runtime, or docs.

Related open beads:
- prompt-language-cva6
- prompt-language-nm4l
- prompt-language-brwd
title: [RESEARCH] Variable semantics: declaration, undefined values, and scope rules

acceptance_criteria:

description:
Question set:
- What data structures do variables really support today?
- Lists exist, but they are represented as JSON array strings in the variable store; is that the right long-term model?
- Should the runtime grow native arrays, objects/maps, or typed values, or stay string-first with small escape hatches?

What:
- Audit the current variable store and list behavior.
- Evaluate tradeoffs of: scalar-only plus JSON strings, native arrays only, arrays plus objects/maps, and typed declarations.
- Recommend whether to pursue richer data structures now, later, or not at all.

Related open beads:
- prompt-language-mewj
- prompt-language-s5cx
title: [RESEARCH] Variable data structures: lists today, maps/objects/types tomorrow

acceptance_criteria:

description:

What:
- Audit current variable visibility in renderFlow, docs, and debugging workflows.
- Identify the highest-friction gaps when understanding variable state during real runs.
- Recommend minimal documentation/tooling improvements versus deeper runtime changes.

Related open bead:
- prompt-language-h2an
title: [RESEARCH] Variable observability and UX: inspectability, provenance, and docs gaps

description:
## Why
The repo now has a coherent bundle of checked-in docs for disciplined memory, Markdown knowledge interop, retrieval, and evaluation alignment, but there is no executable backlog turning that direction into scoped product work. The older memory bead (`prompt-language-7g58`) covers only part of the surface.

## What
1. Turn the checked-in memory/Markdown docs into an implementation-ready backlog.
2. Keep deterministic runtime state, durable memory, advisory Markdown guidance, and retrieval semantics clearly separated.
3. Sequence the work into the smallest slices that can be validated end to end.
4. Make the trust model, checkpoint boundary, and retrieval boundary explicit before the DSL grows further.
notes:
Planning anchors: docs/wip/memory/memory-knowledge-positioning.md, docs/wip/memory/memory-scrutiny-and-risks.md, docs/wip/memory/markdown-agent-memory.md, docs/wip/memory/memory-roadmap.md, docs/wip/memory/memory-source-notes.md. Legacy related bead: prompt-language-7g58.
Imported `prompt-language-plan.zip` to `docs/wip/memory/knowledge-plan/` on 2026-04-10.
The pack reinforces the current memory roadmap rather than creating a separate program: explicit `knowledge:`/`section`, scoped memory, checkpoint-first rollout, retrieval as an abstraction layer, and eval above normal execution.
Use it as a concise companion pack to the main anchors already linked here.
title: Disciplined memory + Markdown interop roadmap

description:
## Why
The current shipped memory surface is useful but too flat. The new planning docs call for scoped memory, strict versus optional reads, TTL/invalidation, and transactional writes so the runtime can stay deterministic instead of quietly trusting stale state.

## What
1. Define the concrete syntax and semantics for memory scopes.
2. Add strict/optional read behavior with explicit failure/default rules.
3. Add TTL/invalidation and transactional write timing.
4. Update parser/runtime/docs/tests together so the behavior is inspectable and not just parser sugar.

## Likely touch points
- `src/application/parse-flow.ts`
- memory store/runtime integration
- docs/reference for `remember` / `memory:`
- parser/runtime tests
title: Scoped memory semantics: strict reads, invalidation, transactional writes

description:
## Why
The checked-in docs propose `knowledge:` declarations and deterministic `section` lookup so Prompt Language can consume Markdown guidance without pretending that docs are the same thing as runtime state.

## What
1. Define the DSL surface for declaring Markdown knowledge sources.
2. Define deterministic section-addressing semantics.
3. Specify how loaded knowledge becomes visible in status/artifacts so it remains inspectable.
4. Update docs/tests together.

## Out of scope
- Semantic/vector retrieval as a default language behavior.
- Treating Markdown guidance as automatically authoritative policy.
title: Markdown knowledge interop: knowledge sources and deterministic section lookup

acceptance_criteria:

description:
## Why
The new memory docs make a stronger claim than the existing backlog: checkpoints, resumability, handoff summaries, and compaction are core runtime behavior, not just extra memory writes. That boundary needs a real product decision before implementation fragments.

## What
1. Define what belongs in execution/session state versus durable memory.
2. Define checkpoint, resumability, handoff-summary, and compaction responsibilities.
3. Reconcile this with existing resume-state backlog so the repo does not grow two overlapping checkpoint models.

close_reason: Already implemented in advanceConditionLoop and advanceIfNode as grounded-by fast path (exit code = verdict, no judge prompt emitted). Verified in code at lines 1642-1662 and 1823-1847.
description:
## Why
The ask keyword (while ask '...', if ask '...') currently asks Claude to give a yes/no verdict via the capture protocol, even when grounded-by is present. This compounds two flakiness sources: Claude's subjective judgment AND the capture protocol. When grounded-by is present, the command's exit code is objective evidence — using it directly makes the condition fully deterministic.

## What
In advanceConditionLoop() and advanceIfNode(), when the ask condition has a groundedBy command: run the command, use exit 0 = true / non-0 = false. Skip the judge prompt entirely. The grounded-by output is no longer just 'evidence for Claude' — it IS the answer.

For pure ask without grounded-by: continue using Claude judgment but with structured output (single word true/false written to file).

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceConditionLoop, advanceIfNode), src/domain/judge-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- ask with grounded-by uses exit code directly
- No judge prompt emitted when grounded-by is present
- Pure ask (no grounded-by) still works with Claude judgment
- Lint warning for ask without grounded-by remains (or escalates)
title: Use grounded-by exit code as deterministic ask verdict — skip Claude judgment

description:
Why: After compaction or session resume, autoAdvanceNodes may re-run a run: node that already executed. Destructive or non-idempotent commands (database migrations, deployments, file deletions) get double-executed.

Note: Must NOT skip nodes inside retry/foreach loops (re-execution is intentional there). Scope to top-level and if-branch contexts only.

Acceptance criteria:
- Non-loop run: nodes skip on resume if already completed
- Loop-body run: nodes always re-execute
- Cached last_stdout/last_exit_code restored from nodeProgress
- Deploy/migration style commands benefit immediately
title: Idempotent run nodes: skip re-execution of successfully-completed nodes on resume

description:
Why: spawn/await is pure fire-and-forget. Children can't coordinate, share intermediate findings, or request help. This enables debate/critique patterns, pipeline patterns, and supervisor patterns.

What: New nodes: `send "target" "message"` emits to target's inbox (.prompt-language-{name}/inbox.jsonl). `receive` blocks until a message arrives. Parent can send to children and receive from them.

Example:
```
spawn "researcher"
  prompt: Research the auth bug
  send "fixer" "${last_stdout}"
end
spawn "fixer"
  receive
  prompt: Fix the bug described in: ${message}
end
await all
```

Acceptance criteria:
- send/receive implement file-based inbox protocol
- Deadlock prevention: receive has configurable timeout
- Message content filtered for security (same as spawn variable filtering)
- receive sets ${message} and ${message_from} auto-variables
title: Inter-child messaging: `send` / `receive` nodes for parent-child and child-child communication

description:
## Why
Variables in prompt-language are global. A foreach iterator variable named 'item' inside a foreach 'item' silently shadows the outer variable. This causes subtle bugs that are invisible at runtime — the outer loop's iterator gets corrupted. The linter can catch this statically.

## What
In lintFlow() / lintNodes(), track a scope stack as the linter descends into loop bodies and branches. When a let or foreach declares a variable name that already exists in an outer scope, emit a warning: 'Variable X shadows outer definition in foreach/while/if'.

Also: after foreach body exhaustion (handleBodyExhaustion), clean up the foreach iterator variable from SessionState to prevent stale values leaking after the loop.

Key files: src/domain/lint-flow.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts (handleBodyExhaustion).

## Acceptance criteria
- Linter warns on variable shadowing in nested scopes
- Warning message includes variable name and scope type
- Foreach iterator cleaned from state after loop completes
- No false positives on non-shadowing variable reuse
title: Add variable shadow warnings to linter for foreach and nested scopes

description:
Why: Any repeated pattern must be copy-pasted. The include directive provides file-level reuse but is not parameterized. As flows grow, boilerplate duplication becomes painful and error-prone.

What: New `define` block that creates a reusable, parameterized flow fragment. Textual expansion (macro), not runtime call stack — parameters substituted at parse time before parseBlock.

Example:
```
define fix-and-test(file, test_cmd)
  prompt: Fix ${file}
  run: ${test_cmd}
  if command_failed
    prompt: Try again — tests still failing
  end
end

flow:
  fix-and-test("app.js", "npm test")
  fix-and-test("utils.js", "npm test")
```

Acceptance criteria:
- define blocks expand inline at parse time
- Parameters are substituted correctly
- Cycle detection prevents recursive macros
- Undefined macro invocation produces lint error
title: Add sub-flows / parameterized macros (`define` blocks)

description:
## Why
State corruption manifests as bizarre behavior far from the corruption site. Runtime invariant checks after each state transition would catch corruption immediately when it happens, with a useful error message.

## What
Implement assertStateInvariants(state: SessionState): void as a pure domain function. Invariants to check:
1. path resolves to a valid node or is one-past-end
2. nodeProgress iteration <= maxIterations for all nodes
3. status === 'active' implies path.length >= 1
4. setExitVariables values are consistent (command_failed != command_succeeded)
5. transitionSeq is present and non-negative
6. No variable name contains the capture nonce substring

Call assertStateInvariants() in autoAdvanceNodes() after each node transition. In production: log violations. In test (NODE_ENV=test): throw.

Key files: src/domain/session-state.ts or new src/domain/assert-invariants.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Pure domain function with zero external deps
- All 6 invariants checked
- Called after each transition in autoAdvanceNodes
- Throws in test environment, logs in production
- Unit tests for each invariant violation scenario
title: Add runtime invariant assertions to catch state machine corruption early

description:
## Why
autoAdvanceNodes() can execute multiple run: commands (with real side effects) before saving state. If the process crashes mid-loop, side effects happened but state reverts to pre-advance. This is the classic split-brain: reality and state diverge. The WAL makes each transition individually recoverable.

## What
Before any state mutation in autoAdvanceNodes(), append a WAL entry to .prompt-language/wal.jsonl with: {seq, timestamp, prevPath, newPath, nodeKind, variables_delta, status}. On loadCurrent(), if a WAL entry exists without a corresponding committed state (seq > last committed seq), replay it to reconstruct the correct state.

New domain type: WalEntry. New port: WalWriter. New adapter: FileWalWriter. Integration in advanceSingleNode() (write entry) and FileStateStore.readCurrent() (check and replay).

Key files: new src/application/ports/wal-writer.ts, new src/infrastructure/adapters/file-wal-writer.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts, src/infrastructure/adapters/file-state-store.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- WAL entry written before each node transition
- On load, uncommitted WAL entries replayed
- WAL compacted after successful save
- No regression on existing state persistence tests
- Crash simulation test: state recovers correctly after mid-loop crash
title: Add Write-Ahead Log (WAL) for state transitions to enable crash recovery

description:
Why:
The recent discussion surfaced a real product question: prompt-language already relies on Claude Code hooks, but expanding toward multi-host hook management risks mixing together three different things: core DSL syntax, host/runtime integration, and cross-host product scope. Claude's lifecycle hooks are stable today, while Codex currently offers AGENTS.md, notifications, JSON event output, and only an experimental native hooks surface.

What:
- Produce a decision note on whether prompt-language should add multi-host lifecycle automation / hook management at all.
- Decide whether this belongs in core DSL syntax, a repo-level config layer, runtime adapters, or should remain host-specific.
- Use Claude Code and Codex CLI as the concrete comparison set.
- Name the near-term recommendation clearly: implement now, track as planned, defer, or decline.

description:
Why: Parallel fan-out (process each file, test each module, review each PR) is the most common multi-agent pattern. Currently requires verbose manual spawn/await boilerplate. A dedicated construct makes it idiomatic.

What: `foreach-spawn item in ${list} model "..."` spawns one child per item (all in parallel), implicitly awaits all, aggregates child results into parent list variable. Desugars to existing foreach + spawn + await all.

Example:
```
let files = run "find src -name '*.test.ts'"
foreach-spawn file in ${files} model "haiku"
  run: npx vitest ${file}
  let result = prompt "Summarize test results"
end
# ${results} is a JSON array of all child result variables
```

Acceptance criteria:
- foreach-spawn desugars to spawn per item + await all
- Child results aggregated into parent list variable
- Bounded by existing DEFAULT_MAX_FOREACH limit
- model parameter optional
title: Fan-out/reduce pattern: `foreach-spawn` + parallel child aggregation

description:
## Why
All variables are stringly-typed. The condition evaluator does implicit numeric coercion — if count is 'pending', ${count} > 5 silently returns null and falls through to command execution. Type annotations let the parser and linter catch these mismatches statically.

## What
Add optional type annotations to let/var declarations:
  let count: int = 0
  let name: string = 'alice'
  let done: bool = false
  let items: list = []

Parse the ': type' after variable name in parseLetLine(). Store declaredType on LetNode. At runtime in updateVariable(), validate assigned value matches declared type (warn on mismatch, don't hard-fail for backward compat). In the linter, warn when comparison operators are used on string-typed variables, or when a list variable is used in a numeric comparison.

Key files: src/domain/flow-node.ts (LetNode), src/application/parse-flow.ts (parseLetLine), src/domain/session-state.ts (updateVariable), src/domain/lint-flow.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Type annotations parsed and stored on LetNode
- Runtime validation warns on type mismatch
- Linter warns on comparison operators with declared string type
- Fully backward compatible (untyped variables behave as before)
- Unit tests for parsing and validation
title: Add gradual typing for let declarations (let x: int = 0)

description:
## Why this issue exists

The Anthropic engineering blog (Mar 2026) demonstrated that generator-evaluator multi-agent architectures produce dramatically better results than solo agents — a working game vs a broken one. The pattern: generator produces work, evaluator critiques against explicit criteria, generator iterates, repeat until approval.

prompt-language's spawn/await supports this in principle but with poor ergonomics: requires separate flow files for each agent, no built-in retry-until-approved, implicit variable naming, verbose orchestration.

## What needs to be done

Add a first-class `review` block (or `evaluate` node) that expresses generator-evaluator loops concisely:

```
flow:
  retry max 5
    prompt: Implement feature X
    review
      criteria: "Has tests? Handles errors? Clean code?"
      grounded-by: npm test
    end
  end
```

The `review` block would:
1. Spawn a skeptical evaluator sub-agent with the criteria as its mandate
2. Give it Playwright/shell access to verify the work
3. Import its verdict (`approved`) and feedback (`critique`) as variables
4. If `approved == false`, inject `critique` into the retry loop's next iteration context
5. If `approved == true`, break out of the retry

Key design decisions:
- Evaluator is intentionally skeptical (prompted to be, unlike self-evaluation)
- Feedback is injected as context for the next generator turn
- The `grounded-by` command gives the evaluator objective ground truth (test results, screenshots)
- Criteria can be inline or referenced from a file

## Acceptance criteria
- Can express a generate-then-review loop in <10 lines of DSL
- Evaluator's critique is visible in flow render output
- Review block works inside retry/while/until loops
- Criteria prompt shapes evaluator behavior (skeptical by default)
- Grounded-by output is provided to evaluator as evidence
- All existing spawn/await tests still pass
title: Feature: review block for generator-evaluator debate loops

description:
## Why
When capture fails repeatedly, retrying the same broken format wastes turns and never recovers. Repeated failures indicate a systematic issue (model confusion, context pollution, nonce mismatch). A circuit breaker detects the pattern and adapts rather than looping forever.

## What
Track captureFailureCount in SessionState. After 5 cumulative capture failures in a session:
- Switch to simplified capture prompt (Write-tool-only, minimal text)
- Log a WARNING in the flow visualization

After 10 cumulative failures:
- Mark flow as failed with diagnostic message explaining the capture system is not working

The counter resets on successful capture.

Key files: src/domain/session-state.ts (add counter), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt), src/domain/capture-prompt.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- captureFailureCount field in SessionState
- Degraded mode prompt at threshold 5
- Flow fails with diagnostic at threshold 10
- Counter resets on success
title: Add capture circuit breaker: detect systemic failures and switch to degraded mode

description:
## Why
Hooks add latency to every Claude Code turn. UserPromptSubmit blocks the response until it completes (60s timeout). If hook processing time grows, the developer experience degrades. Performance regressions should be caught before users notice.

## What (recurring — run before each release)
1. Instrument each hook's wall-clock time (hook entry to exit)
2. Baseline measurements: UserPromptSubmit (state load + autoAdvanceNodes + save), TaskCompleted (gate evaluation), Stop (gate status check)
3. Acceptable thresholds: UserPromptSubmit <500ms for typical 10-node flow, TaskCompleted <2s per gate
4. If any hook exceeds threshold, profile and identify bottleneck
5. State file load/save should be <50ms on typical hardware

## Acceptance criteria
- Baseline measurements documented
- Any above-threshold hooks have profiling results
- Findings documented as comment

## Recurrence
Close and create a new instance before each release.
title: [REVIEW] Hook performance baseline: measure and track latency per hook

description:
## Why
The current capture retry (buildCaptureRetryPrompt) sends essentially the same instruction rephrased. Research shows escalating specificity is more effective than repetition. Repeating a broken format won't fix it — the retry needs to change strategy.

## What
Implement a 3-tier retry strategy parameterized by attempt number:
- Tier 1 (current): rephrase the instruction
- Tier 2: include a concrete example of the expected output (show a Write tool call with example content)
- Tier 3 (last resort): strip all other context, issue a minimal focused prompt with only the capture instruction

buildCaptureRetryPrompt() gains an attemptNumber: number parameter. advanceLetPrompt() passes the attempt count.

Key files: src/domain/capture-prompt.ts (buildCaptureRetryPrompt), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt).

## Acceptance criteria
- buildCaptureRetryPrompt accepts attempt number 1-3
- Each tier produces meaningfully different prompt text
- Attempt count tracked in NodeProgress or passed through state
- All existing capture tests pass
title: Implement tiered escalating retry prompts for capture failures

description:
## Why
The dual-channel capture protocol (inline XML tags + Write-tool file write) is the #1 flakiness source. Claude must produce exactly-formed XML tags in its response, which it frequently fails to do in nested loops, long conversations, and after context compaction. All three domain expert reviewers independently identified this as the highest-ROI change.

## What
Remove the XML tag requirement from buildCapturePrompt() entirely. The sole capture mechanism becomes: Claude writes to .prompt-language/vars/{varName} using the Write tool. Simplify the prompt instruction to just instruct Claude to write its answer to the file. Remove the inline tag extraction logic from advanceLetPrompt() Phase 2 read path.

Key files: src/domain/capture-prompt.ts (buildCapturePrompt), src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt Phase 2), src/presentation/hooks/post-tool-use.ts (scanAndSaveCapturedVars).

## Acceptance criteria
- buildCapturePrompt() emits no XML tag format requirement
- Tag extraction code removed or flagged unused
- All 26 smoke tests pass
- Smoke test N (capture reliability) passes consistently across 5 runs
title: Drop XML tag capture: use Write-tool-to-file as sole capture channel

Example: /flow-eval "${count} > 3 and command_succeeded" shows count=2, 2 > 3 = false, short-circuit: result = false

- Shows intermediate values for each operand
- Works without an active flow (uses last session state)
- Clear error if variable is undefined
title: Add /flow-eval skill: evaluate condition expressions against current variable state

description:
## Why
Throughout the codebase, switch statements on node kinds use default: break which silently ignores unhandled node kinds. This already caused a regression when the continue node was added. TypeScript's never type provides compile-time exhaustiveness checking at zero runtime cost.

## What
Change all default: break patterns in node-kind switches to:
  default: {
    const _exhaustive: never = node;
    throw new Error('Unhandled node kind');
  }

This makes adding a new node kind a compile error until all switch statements handle it.

Key files: src/application/advance-flow.ts (resolveCurrentNode and others), src/domain/lint-flow.ts (lintNodes), src/domain/render-flow.ts, src/application/evaluate-completion.ts.

## Acceptance criteria
- All switch statements on FlowNode.kind use never exhaustiveness
- TypeScript compiles with strict mode
- Adding a new node kind without handling it produces a compile error
title: Add exhaustive node-kind matching (never type) to all switch statements

Note: The + operator for strings needs to be distinguished from numeric addition — best done after typed variables are available.

Acceptance criteria:

- .upper, .lower, .trim, .length work on string/list variables

description:
Why: The DSL currently evaluates all arithmetic and logic left-to-right with no precedence. This silently produces wrong results: `let x = 2 + 3 * 4` yields 20 instead of 14. `if a or b and c` evaluates as (a or b) and c instead of the standard a or (b and c). Every developer carries precedence intuition from every other language they know — violating it creates bugs that are invisible in testing.

What: Implement standard mathematical precedence (* / % before + -) in evaluateArithmetic() and logical precedence (and binds tighter than or) in evaluateCondition(). Both are currently flat left-to-right.

Acceptance criteria:
- let x = 2 + 3 * 4 yields 14
- if a or b and c evaluates as a or (b and c)
- Lint warns on expressions that would evaluate differently under old vs new rules
- All existing tests still pass (add migration warning for changed semantics)
title: Fix operator precedence: arithmetic (* before +) and logic (and before or)

description:
## Why
Currently FileCaptureReader.read() returns null for both 'file not found' (Claude ignored the Write instruction entirely) and 'file empty' (Claude wrote but wrote nothing). These are different failure modes requiring different retry strategies. Better diagnostics enable smarter retry prompts.

## What
Before emitting a capture prompt (advanceLetPrompt Phase 1), after captureReader.clear(), write a sentinel value __PENDING__ to the file. In Phase 2, distinguish:
- File has __PENDING__: Claude did not write to the capture file → emphasize Write tool instruction in retry
- File exists but empty: Claude wrote but wrote nothing → emphasize content requirement in retry
- File has content: success

Add prime(varName) method to CaptureReader port and FileCaptureReader adapter.

Key files: src/application/ports/capture-reader.ts, src/infrastructure/adapters/file-capture-reader.ts, src/application/advance-flow.ts (advanceLetPrompt).

## Acceptance criteria
- CaptureReader port has prime() method
- Sentinel written before each capture prompt
- Phase 2 distinguishes pending vs empty vs content
- Different retry messages for each failure mode
title: Pre-create capture file with sentinel value to enable targeted retry diagnostics

description:
Why: If a hook times out (hung run: command), Claude Code kills the hook process. The state may be partially written — lock acquired, temp file written, but rename not completed. Recovery requires the next hook to detect corruption. A SIGTERM handler can save a consistent snapshot before dying.

What: Add SIGTERM signal handler in each hook entry point that: (1) attempts to save current state, (2) releases lock file, (3) exits cleanly with code 0. Note: SIGTERM is not reliably delivered on Windows (document this limitation).

Acceptance criteria:
- SIGTERM during autoAdvanceNodes saves state and exits cleanly
- Lock file is released (not left as stale)
- Tested on Linux/macOS; Windows limitation documented
- State saved is consistent (not mid-write)
title: Graceful hook timeout escalation: SIGTERM handler saves state before dying

close_reason: Implemented process-group timeout killing, per-command timeout syntax, and timeout audit logging.
description:
Why: The current 30s command timeout in ShellCommandRunner is undocumented and may not kill child processes on all platforms. Node.js exec() sends SIGTERM to the shell but may leave child processes alive. On Windows, timeout behavior differs. A hung npm test or docker build can block the entire hook pipeline.

What: Audit exec() timeout behavior per platform, implement process group kill (SIGTERM to process group, not just shell), document the timeout, add per-command override syntax (`run: cmd timeout 120`), ensure timed-out commands set command_failed=true and last_stderr to 'timed out after Ns'.

Acceptance criteria:
- Process group is killed on timeout (not just the shell)
- Windows: document known limitations, use taskkill /F /T
- Per-command timeout override works in parser
- Audit log includes timeout events
title: Harden per-node timeout: fix process group kill, document behavior, add override syntax
